did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) combined with Discrete Wavelet Transformation (DWT) for feature extraction and classification of skin cancer lesions. The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset to train and evaluate the model, achieving 94% sensitivity and 91% specificity.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'RESEARCH' in the header. It presents original experimental work, proposing a novel approach combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Discrete Wavelet Transformation (DWT) for skin cancer classification. The study utilizes the HAM10000 and ISIC 2018 datasets to train and evaluate the model, reporting specific performance metrics (94% sensitivity, 91% specificity, 94% accuracy) and comparing them against existing methods like ANN and Multilayer Perceptron.

title: Artificial intelligence‑driven enhanced skin cancer diagnosis: leveraging convolutional neural networks with discrete wavelet transformation
journal: Egyptian Journal of Medical Human Genetics
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel skin cancer classification method that integrates Discrete Wavelet Transformation (DWT) for feature extraction with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to enhance diagnostic precision. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset to train and evaluate the model, comparing its performance against Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) and Multilayer Perceptron methods. The primary evaluation approach involved assessing classification metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy on skin lesion images categorized as malignant or benign. The proposed model achieved a sensitivity of 94%, a specificity of 91%, and an overall accuracy of 94%. These results demonstrate that the DWT-based approach outperforms existing machine learning algorithms in accurately classifying skin cancer lesions. The findings suggest that this AI-driven method aids in early detection and diagnosis, potentially improving patient outcomes.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer lesion categories (malignant vs. benign, and seven specific classes: MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is 'skin cancer classification' using CNNs and DWT. It reports classification metrics including sensitivity (94%), specificity (91%), accuracy (94%), and F1-score. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant, and specific lesion types from the HAM10000 dataset).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: The 'Human Against Machine with 10000 training images' dataset, a widely used benchmark for skin cancer research containing dermoscopic images of various skin lesions.

use.training: Used to train the proposed CNN and DWT model.
use.validation: Used to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed model.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed model.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: Dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration used in the study.

use.training: Used to construct the suggested algorithm for detecting skin cancer.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score) and compared against other methods via tabulated values without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed model achieved 94% sensitivity and 91% specificity. The study compares these results against ANN and Multilayer Perceptron baselines. Table 5 lists the proposed Softmax (CNN with DWT) accuracy as 94%, compared to 79.80% for ANN and 70.50% for Multilayer Perceptron.

ISIC 2018.notes: The paper states the ISIC 2018 dataset was used to construct the suggested algorithm for detecting skin cancer. However, specific quantitative results for the proposed model on this dataset are not explicitly reported in the provided text; Table 4 lists ISIC 2018 results for other methods (VGG19_2: 76.6%, CNN: 83.1%) from references [51] and [53] for comparison purposes.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) combined with Discrete Wavelet Transformation (DWT)

components: Discrete Wavelet Transformation (DWT) for feature extraction
architecture_details.description: The architecture integrates DWT-extracted features with a CNN. The CNN consists of hidden layers for feature extraction and fully connected layers for classification. The model utilizes a softmax activation function in the output layer for classification.

classification_or_output_head.details: The softmax function is employed for classifying different types of skin cancers based on the features fed into it.

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing and Standardization: Images resized to a uniform size for consistency.
Noise Reduction and Filtering: Denoising algorithms or filters applied to enhance clarity.
Class Balancing: Techniques such as random oversampling and undersampling used to balance class representation.
Grayscale Conversion: Images processed into grayscale using a median filter to eliminate color traces and reduce computing requirements.
Median Filtering: A nonlinear filtering method used to reduce noise while preserving fine details by replacing each pixel value with the median of its immediate vicinity.
Discrete Wavelet Transformation (DWT): Used for feature extraction, decomposing images into Low-Low (LL), Low-High (LH), High-High (HH), and High-Low (HL) subbands using Haar-based DWT.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Listed as a preprocessing step in the proposed system workflow to enhance the dataset, but specific techniques (e.g., rotation, flipping, scaling) or parameters are not reported in the provided text.

Mentioned as a preprocessing step to ensure balanced representation of different skin lesion classes. The text notes that oversampling minority classes or applying class weights during training might be used to address class imbalances, but does not specify if these were applied or the exact method used.

conclusions: The proposed model combining Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Discrete Wavelet Transformation (DWT) achieved a sensitivity of 94% and specificity of 91% on the HAM10000 dataset.

The proposed CNN with DWT approach outperformed existing machine learning algorithms, specifically Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) with 79.80% sensitivity and Multilayer Perceptron with 70.50% sensitivity.
The integration of DWT for feature extraction with CNNs allows for the leveraging of both spatial and frequency domain information, enhancing classification accuracy.
The DWT-based approach aids in early detection and diagnosis of skin cancer by providing more accurate classification of malignant versus benign lesions.
The study demonstrates that the proposed method improves accuracy compared to existing machine learning algorithms, supporting its viability for enhanced skin cancer diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset for model evaluation, which, while valuable, may not fully represent the global population's skin types and lesion diversity, potentially limiting generalizability.

The paper explicitly acknowledges limitations regarding the trade-off between model complexity and interpretability, noting that increased complexity may hinder clinicians' ability to understand the features driving classifications.

The authors identify significant computational overhead as a limitation, suggesting that the approach may be less feasible for real-time clinical applications due to increased training time and complexity.

The effectiveness of the proposed method is dependent on the quality and appropriateness of the Discrete Wavelet Transformation (DWT) applied, introducing a potential point of failure if transformation quality is suboptimal.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; performance comparisons against baselines (ANN, Multilayer Perceptron) were based on descriptive metrics without statistical significance testing.

The study did not involve external validation on independent clinical cohorts or diverse dermatological settings, relying instead on benchmark datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC 2018) which may not reflect real-world clinical variability.

Specific details regarding data augmentation techniques and class balancing methods are mentioned as necessary steps but are not explicitly detailed in the provided text, leaving potential biases in class representation unverified.
